content: The changing seasons on Earth happen because our planet is tilted as it orbits the Sun. Imagine Earth as a spinning top that leans slightly to one side instead of standing straight up. Because of this tilt, different parts of Earth get more or less sunlight throughout the year. When the northern half of Earth leans toward the Sun, it receives more direct sunlight, making it warmer—this is summer there. At the same time, the southern half leans away from the Sun, getting less sunlight and experiencing winter. Six months later, the situation reverses: the southern half tilts toward the Sun and enjoys summer, while the north has winter. This tilt and orbit create the regular cycle of seasons—spring, summer, fall, and winter—that we experience every year. So, the seasons are not caused by how close Earth is to the Sun, but by which part of Earth is tilted toward or away from it as we travel around the Sun.
